title: The teacher who learned to wait
summary: A Canadian English teacher in Hanoi writes home about what she did not understand at first.
body:
Dear Mum,

I have been in Hanoi for ten months now. I teach English at a small school near Truc Bach lake. I have twenty students. They are between seven and nine years old.

In the first month, I was a bad teacher. I asked a question. Nobody answered. I waited two seconds. Then I gave the answer myself. I thought my students did not understand. I thought they were shy.

A Vietnamese teacher named Linh sat in my class one day. After the class she said, 'You answer too fast.'

I said, 'But they do not speak.'

Linh said, 'They are thinking. In your country, two seconds is a long silence. In ours, two seconds is just the start of thinking. Try seven seconds.'

I tried. The next morning, I asked a question about a small story. I counted in my head: one, two, three, four, five, six, seven. Then a quiet boy in the back raised his hand. He gave a long, careful answer. Then another child spoke. Then another.

I was almost in tears at lunch. Not from sadness. From the feeling that I had been doing it wrong for months.

Now I count to seven in my head before I speak. Sometimes ten. The class is louder than before. But it is a different kind of loud — it is their voices, not mine.

I miss you. The mango trees are starting to flower.

Love,
Chloe

annotations.meaningVi: giơ tay lên — past simple của 'raise'
gần phát khóc — cụm cố định: be in tears = đang khóc
giọng nói của các em, không phải của tôi

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'for' (duration)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Present perfect (have/has + V3) dùng với 'for + khoảng thời gian' để diễn tả hành động bắt đầu trong quá khứ và kéo dài đến hiện tại. Trong bài: 'I have been in Hanoi for ten months now.' Người nói vẫn đang ở Hà Nội. So sánh với past simple ('I was in Hanoi for ten months' — đã rời đi). 'For' đi với độ dài; 'since' đi với điểm bắt đầu (since 2020, since June).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) with 'for + a duration' shows an action that started in the past and continues to now. From the passage: 'I have been in Hanoi for ten months now.' (still there). Compare with past simple: 'I was in Hanoi for ten months' (no longer there). 'For' takes a length; 'since' takes a start point (since 2020, since June).

translationVi:
Mẹ thân yêu,

Con đã ở Hà Nội mười tháng rồi. Con dạy tiếng Anh ở một trường nhỏ gần hồ Trúc Bạch. Con có hai mươi học sinh. Các em từ bảy đến chín tuổi.

Tháng đầu, con là một cô giáo dở. Con đặt một câu hỏi. Không ai trả lời. Con đợi hai giây. Rồi con tự đưa ra câu trả lời. Con tưởng học sinh không hiểu. Con tưởng các em nhút nhát.

Một cô giáo Việt Nam tên Linh ngồi dự một buổi học của con một hôm. Sau giờ học cô nói: 'Em trả lời nhanh quá.'

Con nói: 'Nhưng các em không nói.'

Cô Linh nói: 'Các em đang suy nghĩ. Ở nước em, hai giây là một khoảng im lặng dài. Ở nước cô, hai giây mới chỉ là lúc bắt đầu suy nghĩ. Thử bảy giây xem.'

Con thử. Sáng hôm sau, con đặt một câu hỏi về một mẩu chuyện nhỏ. Con đếm thầm: một, hai, ba, bốn, năm, sáu, bảy. Rồi một cậu bé yên lặng ở phía sau giơ tay. Em ấy đưa ra một câu trả lời dài, cẩn thận. Rồi một em khác lên tiếng. Rồi một em nữa.

Giờ ăn trưa con gần phát khóc. Không phải vì buồn. Vì cảm giác con đã làm sai nhiều tháng qua.

Bây giờ con đếm đến bảy trong đầu trước khi nói. Đôi khi đến mười. Lớp ồn hơn trước. Nhưng đó là kiểu ồn khác — là giọng của các em, không phải của con.

Con nhớ mẹ. Cây xoài bắt đầu ra hoa rồi.

Thương,
Chloe

questions.prompt: How long has Chloe been in Hanoi?
questions.explanation: 'I have been in Hanoi for ten months now.'
'They are between seven and nine years old.'

options: She only waited two seconds, then answered her own questions
questions.explanation: 'I waited two seconds. Then I gave the answer myself.'

questions.prompt: What did Linh explain about silence?
options: In Vietnam two seconds is just the start of thinking; try seven seconds

questions.prompt: What did Chloe do the next morning?
options: Counted to seven in her head before speaking

questions.prompt: Who spoke first after she waited?
options: A quiet boy in the back
questions.explanation: 'A quiet boy in the back raised his hand.'

questions.prompt: Why was Chloe almost in tears at lunch?
options: Because she felt she had been doing it wrong for months
questions.explanation: 'From the feeling that I had been doing it wrong for months.'

questions.prompt: How is the class different now?
options: Louder — but with the students' voices, not the teacher's

questions.prompt: What is happening with the mango trees?
questions.explanation: 'The mango trees are starting to flower.'

options: A teacher's first job in a new culture may be to slow down enough to hear it.
Vietnamese children do not like English.
Foreign teachers should ask fewer questions.
Teaching is the same in every country.
questions.hint: Notice what Chloe changed about herself.

Dear Mum,

I have been in Hanoi for ten {{b1}} now. I teach English at a small school near Truc Bach lake. I have twenty students. They are between seven and nine years old.

In the first month, I was a bad teacher. I asked a question. Nobody {{b2}}. I waited two seconds. Then I gave the answer myself. I thought my students did not understand. I thought they were {{b3}}.

A Vietnamese teacher named Linh sat in my class one day. After the class she said, 'You answer too {{b4}}.'

I said, 'But they do not speak.'

Linh said, 'They are thinking. In your country, two seconds is a long {{b5}}. In ours, two seconds is just the start of thinking. Try seven seconds.'

I tried. The next morning, I asked a question about a small story. I counted in my {{b6}}: one, two, three, four, five, six, seven. Then a quiet boy in the back {{b7}} his hand. He gave a long, careful answer. Then another child spoke. Then another.

I was almost in {{b8}} at lunch. Not from sadness. From the feeling that I had been doing it wrong for months.

Now I count to seven in my head before I speak. Sometimes ten. The class is louder than before. But it is a different kind of loud — it is their voices, not {{b9}}.

I miss you. The mango trees are starting to {{b10}}.

Love,

blanks.explanation: Two seconds is a long silence.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Linh tells Chloe, 'Two seconds is just the start of thinking. Try seven seconds.' Is there a conversation in your life — at work, at home, with a child — where you might be filling silence too quickly? What would you hear if you counted to seven instead?
